text: The surgeon places three 12 mm ports and two 5 mm ports for laparoscopic access. The lesion in the sigmoid colon is identified, and the omentum is moved away from the surgical field. The bowel is positioned on the right side. The inferior mesenteric vein is visualized near the duodenal flexure, serving as a landmark for initiating medial trilateral dissection along the avascular plane between the mesocolic fascia and Gerota's fascia.

The surgeon separates the mesocolon from the pancreas and dissects the inferior mesenteric vein, dividing it with an energy device after applying hemolytic clips. Assisted by visual cues, the inferior mesenteric artery is identified along with its branches, the left colic artery and the superior rectal artery. The peritoneal cuts are made to further expose the inferior mesenteric artery, with the sigmoid colon stretched to reveal the groove formed by the peritoneal fold, facilitating the incision.

The surgeon performs sharp dissection to identify and carefully separate the nerves from the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), preventing post-operative complications. The dissection continues below the IMA to drop down the ureter and gonadal vessels. The nerves are fully separated, and the IMA is isolated at its origin, revealing the superior rectal artery and left colic artery. Fibrofatty tissue and lymph nodes are excised along with the specimen for complete mesocolic excision.
The surgeon continues the dissection inferiorly to initiate the presacral dissection, entering the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane. The nerves are visible on either side within the TME plane.

The surgeon initiates lateral mobilization of the left colon along the line of Toldt, progressing towards the splenic flexure. The lesser sac is opened to facilitate further colon mobilization. The left colic artery is identified, dissected, clipped, and divided, while preserving the superior rectal artery to maintain vascularity to the rectal pouch, especially as the specimen is divided at the promontory.

The surgeon cuts the mesocolon at the distal margin, isolating individual sigmoid branches. Once the distal margin is free, it is divided using a stapler. The surgeon further isolates, ligates, and divides the sigmoid branches to prevent de-vascularization of the rectal pouch. The superior rectal artery is carefully isolated, preserved, and dropped down. The specimen is delivered through an incision in the left iliac fossa with adequate margins.

The surgeon reintroduces the specimen into the peritoneal cavity and performs an end-to-end anastomosis using a circular stapler. The orientation of the colon is confirmed to prevent twisting before completing the anastomosis.
